findings.title: Session prompt now exposes a raw file-exfiltration primitive to every bot conversation
findings.description: The new `session_context` text teaches the model the exact `<!--pidory:attach:...-->` control sequence in every Discord session. That turns file attachment from an opt-in capability into a universal prompt surface: any malicious user message, pasted file, or prompt-injected tool output can now ask the model to emit attachment markers. The current server-side guard in `src/handler/file_attach.rs` only blocks a narrow set of paths, so readable secrets such as repo-local `.env` files under `/home/.../project/.env`, SSH configs outside `$HOME` dotfile root checks, or other non-blocklisted files remain attachable. For a bot, this is a direct data-exfiltration risk.
findings.suggestion: Do not advertise the raw marker syntax in the global session reminder. Keep attachment behind an explicit skill/tool invocation, and validate attachments against an allowlist of user-requested paths or workspace-scoped files instead of the current sparse denylist.

findings.title: Skill sync loop is brittle and can fail or silently miss files
findings.description: `for skill_dir in "$PROJECT_DIR/skills"/*/; do` with `cp -r "$skill_dir"* ...` is unsafe shell globbing. If a skill directory is empty, the `*` is left literal and `cp` exits non-zero under `set -e`, aborting the whole update. Hidden files are also skipped, so a skill that needs dotfiles will be installed incompletely. Because this is the update path, a single malformed or empty skill directory can brick updates.
findings.suggestion: Enable `nullglob` for the loop and copy directory contents with a form that includes dotfiles and tolerates empties, for example `shopt -s nullglob dotglob` plus copying an array of entries, or use `rsync -a --delete "$skill_dir"/ "$SKILLS_TARGET/$skill_name"/`.

findings.title: Installer uses the same unsafe glob-based skill copy logic
findings.description: The new install step repeats the same `cp -r "$skill_dir"*` pattern as `update.sh`. An empty skill directory or one containing only dotfiles will cause `cp` to fail under `set -e`, aborting installation after the service has already been configured. Even when it does not fail, hidden files are omitted, which makes skill deployment incomplete and hard to debug.
findings.suggestion: Use the same fix as the updater: avoid raw `*` expansion, include dotfiles intentionally, and handle empty directories without failing the whole install.

findings.title: The new skill hardcodes GNU-only file inspection commands
findings.description: The skill instructs the model to validate files with `stat -c "%s"`, which works on GNU/Linux but fails on macOS, even though the project explicitly supports macOS elsewhere in this diff. On Darwin the generated Bash step will error before the marker is emitted, so `/pidory-toss` becomes unreliable for a supported platform.
findings.suggestion: Document and use cross-platform commands in the skill, for example `stat -f "%z"` on macOS and `stat -c "%s"` on Linux, or avoid shell-specific `stat` flags entirely by using a portable helper.

summary: The diff adds useful functionality, but it also introduces a high-risk attachment exfiltration surface and two brittle shell deployment paths.
